news_summary: Recent advances highlight women's crucial role in shaping prehistoric Europe, while new agreements safeguard actors' rights against AI in Hollywood.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en lumière le rôle crucial joué par les femmes dans le façonnement de l'Europe préhistorique, tandis que de nouveaux accords protègent les droits des acteurs face à l'intelligence artificielle à Hollywood.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances ponen de relieve el papel fundamental de las mujeres en la configuración de la Europa prehistórica, mientras que los nuevos acuerdos protegen los derechos de los actores frente a la inteligencia artificial en Hollywood.

articles.title: Ancient DNA reveals how women helped transform prehistoric Europe
articles.summary: New DNA evidence shows that Europe’s hunter-gatherers and early farmers interacted far more closely than previously thought, with women likely playing a crucial role in spreading farming across northwestern Europe. Centuries later, the arrival of Bell Beaker migrants triggered another sweeping population transformation that extended all the way to Britain.
articles.content:
When ancient DNA studies began to gain attention, little more than a decade ago, the view took hold among geneticists that everything we thought we knew about the peopling of Europe by modern humans was wrong. The story was simpler than anyone was expecting: Europe was settled in just three massive migrations from the east.

First came the hunter-gatherers, more than 40,000 years ago. Then, after 9,000 years ago, there was an expansion of farming people from Anatolia during the Neolithic age.

Finally, from 5,000 years ago, the Corded Ware people expanded out of the Russian steppe to inaugurate the European bronze age. The Corded Ware were named after the cord-like impressions in their pottery and carried a distinctive genetic signature previously absent from most of Europe. Genetically, most present-day Europeans have some of each.

This was always an over-simplification, however. Our new paper, produced with colleagues from the US and across Europe, has highlighted some of the more complex interactions between ancient populations that took place in north-west Europe.

Our research untangles the origins of prehistoric populations across Belgium and the Netherlands, as well as identifying the source population for a migration into Britain during the late Neolithic that seems to have led to a 90% replacement of Britain’s Neolithic farmers.

Ancient DNA research already suggested a much more nuanced picture. For example, when early Neolithic farmers first moved into Europe, they interacted little with the local hunter-gatherer people. As a result, although they now lived far from their homeland, their genomes still resembled those of their ancestors from Anatolia.

But by 1,000–2,000 years later, they had absorbed significant local ancestry. Their hunter-gatherer ancestry swelled from only 10% to 30–40% in some regions. Clearly the hunter-gatherers had not vanished as the farmers expanded.

Northern wetlands

The new research takes us even further from the simple picture. Almost a decade ago, our research group at the University of Huddersfield began a collaboration with palaeoecologist Professor John Stewart from Bournemouth University and archaeologists at the Université de Liège, Belgium. We analysed the genomes of Neolithic human remains excavated along the River Meuse in Belgium, dating to around 5,000 years ago.

This work became part of a larger project, led by Professor David Reich and Dr Iñigo Olalde at Harvard University, involving geneticists and archaeologists from across western Europe. This widened the focus to further sites around the Lower Rhine–Meuse area – wetlands and coastal areas as well as rivers – spanning the late hunter-gatherer cultures to the bronze age.

The fertile soils south of the Rhine-Meuse wetlands had attracted pioneer Neolithic farmer-colonists as early as 5,500BC. However, the rich resources of the northern wetlands were more suited to the lifestyle practised by hunter-gatherers. Even so, the results, generated by our research student, Alessandro Fichera, in collaboration with Harvard, came as a big surprise.

The genomes of people from later Neolithic times in Belgium carried at least 50% local hunter-gatherer ancestry, alongside the expected Anatolian farmer ancestry. Discussing these results with our collaborators led to a “eureka” moment: the same pattern appeared at other sites situated in similarly water-rich environments across the region.

Notably, many of the earlier Neolithic Dutch samples from further north – such as the Swifterbant culture, well-known for maintaining a hunter-gatherer economy alongside some adoption of agriculture – carried close to 100% hunter-gatherer ancestry.

Women’s role in the spread of farming

We then compared the Y-chromosome and mitochondrial DNA, which track the male and female lines of descent, respectively. The Y chromosomes in the Belgian remains were all characteristic of hunter-gatherers, but three-quarters of the mitochondrial DNA lineages had come from Neolithic farmers living further south. The implication was clear: farming know-how had been imported into the “waterworld” hunter-gatherer communities by women.

Our findings support a version of the “frontier mobility” or “availability” model for the spread of the Neolithic, proposed by archaeologists Marek Zvelebil and Peter Rowley-Conwy in the 1980s. They envisioned a contact zone between pioneer farming groups arriving by “leapfrog colonisation” and hunter-gatherer areas.

In the model, the “availability” phase entailed contact and small-scale movements across the frontier, with trading relationships and marriage alliances, for example, forming gradually. This would be followed by a “substitution” phase where farming develops alongside foraging in the hunter-gatherer area, and eventually a “consolidation” phase, when farming predominates.

Our results suggest that the frontier was much more permeable to women than it was to men, and that it may have been marriage of Neolithic women into the forager communities that eventually helped the hunter-gatherers to adopt farming full time. After all, because of the predominance of farming across Europe, the likely alternative long-term was extinction.

Perhaps this kind of model might also apply to other parts of Europe where we lack evidence for how the increased hunter-gatherer ancestry in the later Neolithic came about. In any case, the fact that, here, the “more advanced” farming women married into hunter-gatherer groups, contrary to many archaeologists’ expectations that hunter-gatherer women would “marry up”, suggests that perceptions need to change.

Beakers, bronze age and Britain

Around 4,600 years ago, though, people were on the move again. A new wave of settlers – pastoralist-farmers hailing ultimately from the Russian steppe – began to infiltrate the Rhine area in the form of the Corded Ware culture. As growing numbers moved in from the east, they were transformed – we still don’t understand exactly how – into what is known as the Bell Beaker culture.

Within a few centuries, the genetic landscape of the Rhine-Meuse region, including the wetlands, was completely reshaped. Our colleagues found that, 4,400 years ago, less than 20% of the ancestry of the people living there traced back to the earlier farmers and hunter-gatherers. At least 80% of their ancestry was now from the steppe.

The Bell Beaker people rapidly expanded and rippled out further in all directions, creating the bronze age of central Europe. And not only central Europe – they also spread across the English Channel and throughout Britain, extending as far north as Orkney.

It looks as if the British farmers who had been building Stonehenge over the preceding centuries all but disappeared – again, for reasons which remain unclear.

But did they actually vanish? Perhaps this rather blunt picture might become more nuanced too, as we learn more fine-grained details of what happened from archaeology and ancient DNA.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding prehistoric European population dynamics, highlighting the crucial role of women in spreading farming and cultural transformation. This research enriches our knowledge of human history, challenges previous assumptions, and promotes a more nuanced view of gender roles in ancient societies, which has broad educational and cultural significance.
articles.personality_title: Ancient DNA shows women helped spread farming in prehistoric Europe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have studied ancient DNA to learn how early people lived and moved across Europe. For a long time, they thought Europe’s population was shaped by just a few big migrations from the east.

**What happened** – New research looked at DNA from ancient human remains in Belgium and the Netherlands. It found that women from early farming communities moved into hunter-gatherer groups living near wetlands. These women brought farming skills, mixing their DNA with local people. Later, a big new group called the Bell Beaker people arrived from the east, changing the population again and spreading into Britain.

**Impact** – This study shows that women played a key role in spreading farming by joining hunter-gatherer communities. It also reveals how early European populations mixed more than we thought. This changes old ideas about how farming spread and who influenced prehistoric societies.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to study more ancient DNA to understand better how these population changes happened. They want to learn if similar patterns of movement and mixing happened in other parts of Europe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ancient DNA reveals that farming spread in prehistoric Europe largely through women joining hunter-gatherer groups, reshaping how we understand early human history.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ADN ancien révèle que les femmes ont aidé à répandre l’agriculture en Europe préhistorique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont étudié l’ADN ancien pour mieux comprendre comment les premiers peuples vivaient et se déplaçaient en Europe. Pendant longtemps, on pensait que la population européenne s’était formée grâce à quelques grandes migrations venant de l’est.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – De nouvelles recherches ont analysé l’ADN d’anciens restes humains en Belgique et aux Pays-Bas. Elles ont découvert que des femmes issues des premières communautés agricoles ont rejoint des groupes de chasseurs-cueilleurs vivant près des zones humides. Ces femmes ont apporté les techniques agricoles, mélangeant leur ADN avec celui des populations locales. Plus tard, un grand groupe appelé les Bell Beaker est arrivé de l’est, modifiant encore la population et s’étendant jusqu’en Grande-Bretagne.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que les femmes ont joué un rôle clé dans la diffusion de l’agriculture en rejoignant les groupes de chasseurs-cueilleurs. Elle révèle aussi que les populations européennes anciennes se sont mélangées plus qu’on ne le pensait. Cela change les idées anciennes sur la façon dont l’agriculture s’est répandue et qui a influencé les sociétés préhistoriques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent étudier plus d’ADN ancien pour mieux comprendre comment ces changements de population ont eu lieu. Ils veulent savoir si des schémas similaires de mouvements et de mélanges ont eu lieu dans d’autres régions d’Europe.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ADN ancien montre que l’agriculture s’est surtout répandue en Europe préhistorique grâce aux femmes qui ont rejoint des groupes de chasseurs-cueilleurs, changeant notre compréhension de l’histoire humaine ancienne.
articles.personality_title_es: El ADN antiguo muestra que las mujeres ayudaron a difundir la agricultura en la Europa prehistórica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado ADN antiguo para entender mejor cómo vivían y se movían las primeras personas en Europa. Durante mucho tiempo, se pensó que la población europea se formó por unas pocas grandes migraciones desde el este.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevas investigaciones analizaron el ADN de restos humanos antiguos en Bélgica y los Países Bajos. Descubrieron que mujeres de las primeras comunidades agrícolas se unieron a grupos de cazadores-recolectores que vivían cerca de zonas húmedas. Estas mujeres trajeron conocimientos agrícolas y mezclaron su ADN con el de la población local. Más tarde, un gran grupo llamado los Bell Beaker llegó desde el este, cambiando la población y llegando hasta Gran Bretaña.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que las mujeres tuvieron un papel clave en la difusión de la agricultura al unirse a grupos de cazadores-recolectores. También revela que las poblaciones antiguas europeas se mezclaron más de lo que se pensaba. Esto cambia las ideas antiguas sobre cómo se difundió la agricultura y quién influyó en las sociedades prehistóricas.

**Próximos pasos** – Los investigadores esperan estudiar más ADN antiguo para entender mejor cómo ocurrieron estos cambios poblacionales. Quieren saber si patrones similares de movimiento y mezcla ocurrieron en otras partes de Europa.

**Frase clave** – El ADN antiguo revela que la agricultura se difundió en la Europa prehistórica principalmente gracias a mujeres que se unieron a grupos de cazadores-recolectores, cambiando nuestra comprensión de la historia humana antigua.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a flowing river merging two distinct landscapes: on one side, stylized pottery with cord-like patterns and steppe grasslands representing the Corded Ware and Bell Beaker cultures, and on the other, a mosaic of intertwined female silhouettes formed from delicate wheat stalks and fishing nets symbolizing Neolithic farming women integrating with hunter-gatherer communities in the wetlands of the Rhine-Meuse region.

articles.title: Hollywood studios, actors' union agree on protections against AI
articles.summary: Hollywood studios have found common ground with union leaders on a newly negotiated agreement that was approved by the national board last month and is currently being voted on by members. New protections were introduced to control the use of artificial intelligence by production companies, potentially avoiding another damaging strike like the one that shuttered the industry in 2023.
articles.content:
SAG-AFTRA chief negotiator Duncan Crabtree-Ireland attributed the mostly drama-free agreements with studios during this round of negotiations to a new mindset.

As Hollywood's performers cast their ballots to approve the latest negotiated contract, union leaders say they have made some progress in conversations with studio bosses since the massive strike in 2023, especially when it comes to concerns about artificial intelligence.

SAG-AFTRA chief negotiator Duncan Crabtree-Ireland attributed the mostly drama-free agreements in this round of negotiations to a new mindset, "because the studios and streamers came to the table with a different perspective."

With 160,000 members working in film, television and video games, SAG-AFTRA is the largest and most influential union of its kind globally.

Members of the actors' union are voting on a newly negotiated agreement that was approved by the national board earlier this month, ahead of the current contract's expiration at the end of June.

Read morePalme d’Or winner Cristian Mungiu on the film that conquered Cannes

"The tone of the negotiation was much more collaborative and a lot more creativity was brought by both sides, so I really believe that the 2023 strikes – while they were very difficult for all of us – did help effectuate a reset in the relationship between the studios and the unions in general," Crabtree-Ireland said.

Approval would mean avoiding a repeat of disastrous 2023 strikes that shuttered productions, costing studios billions of dollars, while actors stood their ground against AI and other issues.

AI's evolutions

Actor Kevin Bacon joined SAG-AFTRA members and supporters during the 2023 strike in front of Paramount Studios in New York City. © Michael loccisano , Getty Images North America/ AFP

The strike by the Screen Actors Guild-American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (SAG-AFTRA) lasted 118 days, with star-studded picket lines outside major studios in Los Angeles and New York, marking the longest such revolt in Hollywood history.

AI technology was a sticking point for many, and that tension persists, Crabtree-Ireland said.

"They do feel more secure than they did in 2023 but there's still a very, very strong concern about AI – and especially because the generative AI tools have advanced so much in the last three years," he said.

The latest agreement does not close the door on AI, but it does introduce new protections.

Under the new contract, digital replicas – which use AI or any technology to replicate an actual living or deceased performer – must "have informed consent and fair compensation," Crabtree-Ireland said.

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again arts24 © FRANCE 24 12:50

The contract allows for limited use of synthetics, under "unusual circumstances," when a generative AI system can be used to create a character who is not based on any actual person in the world.

"There's now process in place which would require the companies to come to the union if they want to use a synthetic in a project, they have to demonstrate to us that this synthetic brings a significant additional value to the production," Crabtree-Ireland said.

"While this doesn't rise quite to the level of a complete prohibition, it's a very strong disincentive."

Voting on the latest contract closes June 4.

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant agreement between Hollywood studios and the actors' union that introduces protections against unregulated AI use, ensuring informed consent and fair compensation for performers. This agreement addresses broad concerns about AI's impact on workers' rights and aims to prevent disruptive strikes, benefiting a large workforce and setting a precedent for AI governance in creative industries.
articles.personality_title: Hollywood actors and studios agree on new rules to protect against AI misuse
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Hollywood actors and studios have had tough negotiations in the past, especially with a long strike in 2023 that stopped many productions. One big worry has been how artificial intelligence (AI) might affect actors’ work and rights.

**What happened** – The actors’ union, called SAG-AFTRA, and Hollywood studios reached a new agreement that includes clear rules about using AI. This deal was approved by the union’s national board and is now being voted on by its 160,000 members before the current contract ends in June.

**Impact** – This agreement is important because it protects actors from having their image or work copied by AI without permission or fair payment. It also stops studios from using AI-generated characters based on real people unless they get approval and show it helps the project a lot. These rules aim to avoid another big strike like in 2023 and make sure actors feel safer about AI’s growing role.

**What’s next step** – Union members will finish voting by June 4 to decide if they accept the new contract. If approved, the industry may avoid more strikes and set an example for how AI should be handled in movies and TV shows.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Hollywood’s actors and studios have agreed on new protections that control AI use to protect performers’ rights and prevent future work stoppages.
articles.personality_title_fr: Acteurs et studios d’Hollywood s’accordent sur des règles pour protéger contre l’usage abusif de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les acteurs et les studios d’Hollywood ont connu des négociations difficiles, notamment avec une longue grève en 2023 qui a stoppé de nombreuses productions. Une grande inquiétude concernait l’impact de l’intelligence artificielle (IA) sur le travail et les droits des acteurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le syndicat des acteurs, appelé SAG-AFTRA, et les studios ont conclu un nouvel accord avec des règles claires sur l’utilisation de l’IA. Cet accord a été approuvé par le conseil national du syndicat et est maintenant soumis au vote des 160 000 membres avant la fin du contrat actuel en juin.

**Impact** – Cet accord est important car il protège les acteurs contre la reproduction de leur image ou travail par l’IA sans permission ni rémunération équitable. Il interdit aussi l’usage de personnages générés par IA basés sur de vraies personnes sans accord préalable et justification. Ces règles visent à éviter une nouvelle grande grève comme en 2023 et à rassurer les acteurs face au rôle croissant de l’IA.

**Prochaine étape** – Les membres du syndicat voteront jusqu’au 4 juin pour accepter ou non ce nouveau contrat. S’il est approuvé, l’industrie pourrait éviter d’autres arrêts de travail et montrer l’exemple sur la gestion de l’IA dans le cinéma et la télévision.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les acteurs et studios d’Hollywood ont convenu de protections encadrant l’usage de l’IA pour défendre les droits des artistes et éviter les grèves futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Actores y estudios de Hollywood acuerdan nuevas reglas para protegerse del mal uso de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los actores y estudios de Hollywood han tenido negociaciones difíciles, especialmente con una larga huelga en 2023 que detuvo muchas producciones. Una gran preocupación era cómo la inteligencia artificial (IA) podría afectar el trabajo y los derechos de los actores.

**Qué pasó** – El sindicato de actores, llamado SAG-AFTRA, y los estudios llegaron a un nuevo acuerdo que incluye reglas claras sobre el uso de la IA. Este acuerdo fue aprobado por la junta nacional del sindicato y ahora está siendo votado por sus 160,000 miembros antes de que termine el contrato actual en junio.

**Impacto** – Este acuerdo es importante porque protege a los actores para que su imagen o trabajo no sean copiados por IA sin permiso ni pago justo. También impide que los estudios usen personajes generados por IA basados en personas reales sin aprobación y demostrando que aportan mucho al proyecto. Estas reglas buscan evitar otra huelga grande como la de 2023 y que los actores se sientan más seguros con el creciente uso de la IA.

**Próximo paso** – Los miembros del sindicato votarán hasta el 4 de junio para decidir si aceptan el nuevo contrato. Si se aprueba, la industria podría evitar más huelgas y dar un ejemplo sobre cómo manejar la IA en cine y televisión.

**Resumen en una frase** – Actores y estudios de Hollywood acordaron nuevas protecciones para controlar el uso de la IA y proteger los derechos de los artistas, evitando paros laborales futuros.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two hands—one made of film reels and vintage cameras, the other composed of softly glowing digital circuits—gently shaking over a classic Hollywood studio backdrop with a peaceful sky, symbolizing the harmonious agreement between actors’ union and studios on AI protections.
